core_intuitions: The tool schema lives in the model's CONTEXT (read-only input), while the tool CALL is text the model GENERATES — this asymmetry is the whole mechanism of function calling.

The tool-call loop is a cycle: model call -> if tool calls, execute and append results -> call again; it exits only on a plain-text response or a max-iteration cap, and the message history grows O(n) in the number of calls.

The harness turns raw model text into an executed call via a parse -> validate -> dispatch pipeline, and EVERY failure at every stage takes the same path back to the model: errors are just tool results.

Function calling is a learned META-SKILL taught by SFT with loss only on the assistant turns (the tool-call JSON and the grounded final answer); because the schema is in-context, the skill generalizes to tools never seen in training.
Structured outputs (schema-constrained decoding) and function calling are complementary: one guarantees valid JSON shape, the other governs whether/which tool to invoke.

existing.name: toolcall-loop-architecture
existing.issue: The loop cycle reads clearly in light mode, but dark mode has real legibility failures: the bottom caption ('Loop exits when...') and the 'iteration < max_iterations' label render near-black on the dark background, and the green 'final text response -> return' text is faint (low-contrast green on dark green). These are hardcoded/non-theme-aware fills and text colors rather than theme foreground tokens.
existing.improvement: Make all text and edge labels theme-aware: title, caption, node labels, and the 'iteration < max_iterations' label should use the theme foreground color so they read in both themes. Give the success/'no tool calls' green accent enough contrast on a dark background (or draw its text in the theme foreground and reserve green for the stroke only). Verify the near-white node fills still contrast their text in dark mode. Keep the layout and content unchanged — only fix theme safety.

new_figures.name: tool-call-anatomy
new_figures.concept: Where the schema lives (read-only context the model never edits) vs where the call is produced (generated text), and the round-trip through the harness back into context.
new_figures.rationale: This is the single unifying insight the chapter states explicitly ('the schema is in the context and the call is in the generated text', line 89) but never draws. Every downstream idea — why the skill generalizes to unseen tools, why errors are fed back, why context grows — follows from grasping this input/output asymmetry. It is the mental model the existing loop figure abstracts away.
new_figures.placement: End of the 'How schemas reach the model' subsection, after the paragraph noting Llama/Mistral/Claude formats and the 'schema is in the context / call is in the generated text' sentence (around line 89).
new_figures.brief: A two-zone diagram making the input/output asymmetry unmistakable. LEFT (or TOP) zone titled 'Context — input the model READS (never edits)': a vertical stack of three labeled blocks — (1) system prompt, (2) TOOL SCHEMAS, drawn as a small JSON card showing 'name / description / parameters(type, enum, required)', visually emphasized as the key block, (3) conversation so far. RIGHT (or BOTTOM) zone titled 'Generation — text the model WRITES': a single block showing a wrapped tool-call span, e.g. <tool_call>{"name": ..., "arguments": {...}}</tool_call>, with the JSON shape clearly echoing the schema's fields. Draw a prominent arrow from the TOOL SCHEMAS block to the generated call labeled 'conditions / constrains the generated JSON' (the schema tokens shape what the model emits). Then show the harness as a small gear/box between generation and context that (a) EXTRACTS the call from the generated text, (b) runs the tool, and (c) feeds the result BACK as a new 'tool' message that appends into the Context zone's conversation block — draw this as a curved return arrow labeled 'result appended -> becomes context'. The takeaway to make legible in one glance: the schema is fixed read-only input; the call is freshly written output; the harness closes the loop by turning outputs back into inputs. Use only tiny illustrative field names, no benchmark numbers. Theme-safe: node fills and text via theme tokens; reserve one accent for the 'conditions/constrains' arrow. Everything must fit the viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading order and no overlapping text.

new_figures.name: parse-validate-dispatch-pipeline
new_figures.concept: The harness pipeline that converts raw model text into an executed tool call, and how failures at every stage converge onto the same feedback channel — errors are tool results.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter says 'this is where most production bugs live' and 'the key insight: errors are tool results.' A code listing plus an error-taxonomy table communicate the parts but not the shape: that success and every distinct failure mode take ONE common path back into the conversation. A static pipeline with converging error arrows makes that architectural insight click far faster than prose.
new_figures.placement: Under 'Parsing and Validation', after the 'parse-validate-dispatch pipeline' code block and around the 'Error taxonomy' subsection (lines 136-249).
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right pipeline of four processing stages, each a labeled box: [Raw model text] -> 'Extract' (regex finds <tool_call>...</tool_call>) -> 'Parse JSON' (json.loads) -> 'Validate' (tool exists? args conform to schema?) -> 'Dispatch' (call the Python fn) -> [Result]. Below each of the four stages, draw a small downward 'failure' arrow in a single semantic error accent (not a hardcoded red hex — use a theme-safe error token) labeled with that stage's characteristic failure: Extract -> 'no tool call found'; Parse JSON -> 'JSON syntax error (trailing comma, single quotes, True/None)'; Validate -> 'unknown tool / missing required arg / wrong type'; Dispatch -> 'execution error / timeout / API 500'. All four failure arrows, plus the success 'Result' from the end of the pipeline, converge into ONE shared node labeled 'tool message appended to conversation'. From that node draw a single return arrow back to the model (a small 'LLM' glyph) labeled 'model sees it and can self-correct next turn'. The dominant visual message, legible at a glance: success and every failure mode flow through the SAME channel back to the model — 'errors are tool results.' Keep labels terse; no invented numbers. Theme-safe fills/text; error accent must contrast in both light and dark. Ensure the converging arrows do not cross-clutter — route them cleanly into the shared node with a clear reading order.

new_figures.name: sft-loss-masking-tool-use
new_figures.concept: How the function-calling meta-skill is taught: a multi-turn SFT example where the loss is computed only on the assistant's tool-call JSON and final grounded answer, while system/user/tool turns are masked context.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter stresses that the capability is a learned meta-skill and that 'loss is computed only on the assistant tokens.' Which tokens are trained vs. fixed context is exactly the kind of thing readers get wrong, and it explains WHY the skill generalizes to unseen tools (the schema is context, not memorized). A single annotated frame of the masked conversation conveys this better than the prose + code.
new_figures.placement: Under 'The Function-Calling Fine-Tune', in/after the 'Training data format' subsection (lines 99-115).
new_figures.brief: A vertical stack of the five turns of one training example, each a labeled row: (1) 'system' — carries the TOOL SCHEMAS (small JSON glyph); (2) 'user' — the query; (3) 'assistant' — the tool call <tool_call>{name, arguments}</tool_call>; (4) 'tool' — the returned result; (5) 'assistant' — the grounded final answer. Visually split the rows into two classes: rows 1,2,4 rendered DIMMED with a right-margin tag 'masked — fixed context, no loss'; rows 3 and 5 rendered HIGHLIGHTED with a tag 'loss computed here — the model learns to generate these tokens.' Add two small callouts: on row 3, 'learns WHEN to call + WHICH tool + how to form the argument JSON'; on row 5, 'learns to ground the answer in the tool result.' At the bottom, a one-line takeaway strip: 'Schema lives in the (masked) context -> the model learns the meta-skill, not specific tool names -> it generalizes to unseen tools.' Use only tiny illustrative content (e.g. a calculate tool), no benchmark numbers. Theme-safe: use opacity/foreground tokens for the dim-vs-highlight distinction and one accent for the highlighted (trained) rows so it reads in both light and dark. Keep the vertical reading order obvious and all tags inside the viewBox.

summary: The chapter is concept-rich but figure-poor: one solid loop-architecture figure exists (refine for dark-theme text legibility), while the chapter's true unifying insight — schema-in-context vs call-in-generated-text — is stated in prose but never drawn. Proposed 3 static figures, ranked: (P1) tool-call anatomy showing the input/output asymmetry and the harness round-trip; (P2) the parse-validate-dispatch pipeline where all failures converge onto one feedback channel ('errors are tool results'); (P3) the SFT loss-masking view that explains why the capability is a generalizing meta-skill. All static — no motion is essential here since each mechanism is fully conveyed by a single well-labeled frame.
